name: Aarya Khanna
about: I'm a data scientist passionate about using data and machine learning techniques to make an impact in helping solve critical problems. My research interests include high-dimensional statistics, clustering, and manifold learning.

highlights: Built classification algorithms and data pipelines to predict and optimize flavor performance using ingredient ratios and trial metadata.
Restructured trial processes to boost statistical power by blocking on key features correlating with historical flavor preferences.
Used I-optimal trial selection to identify which trials to prioritize for maximum information gain and reduced uncertainty in the model feedback loop.
experience.software: Python (Pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn, matplotlib), SQL, Microsoft Office (Excel, Word, PowerPoint)
experience.methods: XGBoost/SHAP, Random Forest, logistic/linear regression, K-means clustering, ANOVA/chi-square tests, regularization, dimension reduction (UMAP, PCA)

experience.organization: UCLA Artificial Intelligence & Cultural Heritage Laboratory
highlights: Clustered questions from 15,000+ large-scale text documents into groups of semantic similarity, leveraging automated pronoun disambiguation.
Built a hybrid pipeline: initial embedding screenings to filter unlikely matches, then a cross-attention model with the right context window to maximize cluster assignment quality.

Automated cluster quality evaluation by comparing each vector’s distance to its cluster centroid—reverse-engineering a “cluster theme”—then used it as a baseline for an LLM to grade compactness with contextual reasoning.
experience.software: Python (PyTorch, Pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn, matplotlib, NLTK, Hugging Face, SBERT), Linux
experience.methods: K-means / HDBSCAN / DBSCAN clustering, logistic/linear regression, LLMs, API text embeddings, dimension reduction (UMAP/PCA), silhouette scores

programming.languages: Python, SQL, R, C++, Java, Tableau, MATLAB
programming.libraries: TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn, Pandas, NumPy, LangChain, Pinecone, Streamlit, SBERT/OpenAI

projects.title: Transit Deserts: Who the Los Angeles Metro System Leaves Behind
bullets: LA County screening: job accessibility, desert mapping, rail corridor ranking.
Census + LEHD + GTFS; Laplacian pseudoinverse and effective resistance.
Heuristic score not a ridership or engineering model.

intro: This project is a reproducible planning screen for Los Angeles County. It estimates tract-level job accessibility by a simplified transit skim, labels high-need tracts, and ranks candidate station-to-station segments using network structure and local activity.

sections.title: Purpose
bullets: Where is cumulative job accessibility by transit (≤30 min) systematically low?
Which tracts sit in the lower tail of that distribution (“transit deserts”) for this snapshot?
Which high-activity station pairs look promising under endpoint demand × effective resistance on today’s rail graph?

sections.title: Data
bullets: Geography: LA County census tracts; consistent map bbox across figures.
Employment: LEHD WAC jobs by tract; ACS for population and median income.
Transit: GTFS feeds full stop set for accessibility skims; rail-like routes for the network graph.

sections.title: Accessibility & transit deserts
bullets: Nearest-stop skim: walk legs, in-vehicle great-circle time at an effective speed, fixed wait no mode choice or timetable routing.
Accessibility = sum of jobs reachable under a 30-minute OD time cap (cumulative opportunity).
Deserts: tracts at or below the 20th percentile of tract accessibility (relative to the modeled county).

segments.text: Notation:
is total OD time; jobs enter the sum where

paragraphs: Figure 1 pairs the accessibility surface with the binary desert layer derived from that percentile rule.

figures.alt: Tract accessibility and transit desert maps
figures.caption: Figure 1   Countywide accessibility (left) and desert mask (bottom 20%). This is the “need” view before corridor scoring.

sections.title: Rail graph & effective resistance
paragraphs: Stops from rail-family GTFS routes define nodes; consecutive stops along trips are edges (meters). Transfer links connect nearby platforms. The largest connected component is retained.

projects.title: TwistEd
bullets: Real-time weather alerts and safest recommended travel routes.
Interactive chatbot for historical weather questions.
Educational section on severe weather types and precautions.

intro: TwistEd combines live hazard data with routing and an educational layer so users get alerts, safer paths, and context on what the risk actually means.
The assistant path uses retrieval over historical weather records so answers stay grounded in real events rather than generic text.
detail.stack: Client requests weather alerts and route preferences; backend aggregates API feeds, scores edges on risk, and serves a small RAG-style chat index over curated historical snippets.
detail.methods: Heuristic or learned route scoring under alerts; optional NLP intent routing for the chatbot; structured cards for the education module.
frameworks: Vector store or BM25 for chat retrieval

visuals.caption: UI mock or alert + route screenshot (add file under /images/).

projects.title: CoursePlanner AI Chatbot
bullets: Uses UCLA’s Developer API with LangChain and Pinecone to build optimized course plans from natural language.
Contextualizes top matches when justifying specific course recommendations.

intro: Students describe goals and constraints in natural language; the system queries UCLA course data, embeds descriptions, and retrieves tight candidate sets before the LLM explains tradeoffs.
Pinecone stores embedding-indexed course chunks so recommendations stay specific to UCLA offerings and update when the catalog changes.
detail.stack: LangChain orchestration (tools for catalog lookup), embedding model for course text, Pinecone for similarity, and a policy prompt that forces citations to retrieved rows.
detail.methods: Top-k retrieval with MMR or diversity, optional re-ranking, and structured JSON outputs for “plan” objects the UI can render.

visuals.caption: Example chat turn with retrieved course cards (screenshot).

projects.title: Analogy LLM
bullets: Building an LLM that explains mathematical concepts in the context of another field through strong analogies.

intro: The goal is pedagogy: map abstract math to a domain the learner already trusts (biology, economics, music, etc.) while preserving definitions and not overstating isomorphism.
Training / prompting strategy centers on analogy quality rubrics—faithfulness, clarity, and appropriate scope—rather than fluency alone.
detail.methods: Instruction tuning or few-shot templates with critique steps; optional tool use for symbolic checks when available.

projects.title: Vertical Los Angeles
bullets: Continuous height surface from footprints: variogram, universal kriging, and uncertainty-aware cluster potential on a metric grid.
Regression-kriging with covariate trend (HistGradientBoosting) plus residual kriging; LEHD workplace jobs overlaid on the same grid.
Descriptive geostatistics plus labor-market indices, not a structural urban model or causal identification stack.

intro: Vertical LA models building height as a spatially continuous field over Los Angeles: empirical variograms, kriging on a regular grid, and derived indices for skyline-like potential. An optional regression-kriging stack adds zoning, transit, and census covariates, then layers LEHD workplace jobs to relate built-form potential to employment dynamics.

The work is built for portfolio-style technical communication: reproducible pipelines, explicit design choices, and maps that separate prediction from variance. It is not a land-use optimizer, structural urban model, or substitute for policy process.

sections.title: Purpose

segments.text: Estimate a smooth predicted height field
with geostatistical uncertainty, not only scattered footprints.

bullets: Quantify spatial dependence (variogram), predict on a grid (kriging), and validate with cross-validation RMSE.

segments.text: Turn predictions plus variance into interpretable indices: expected skyline zones, cluster potential in
, and (in the advanced path) tension between modeled potential and existing tall-building density.

bullets: Optionally overlay LEHD job counts and simple per-cell trends to ask where job growth and level align with high cluster potential.

sections.title: Data & execution
bullets: Building footprints with HEIGHT; centroids in EPSG:32611 (UTM 11N, meters) for meaningful distances and variogram lags.
LA County zoning (WKT), optional GTFS rail distances, optional Census tract covariates via cenpy.
LEHD WAC workplace jobs by block, crosswalked to coordinates and binned to the regression-kriging grid for spatiotemporal layers.
Large raw inputs are gitignored in the repo; tracked artifacts are code plus selected figures under `output/` on GitHub.

sections.title: Variogram & spatial dependence

segments.text: Empirical semivariogram (GSTools), with lag caps and subsampling for large
; multiple candidate models (Exponential, Spherical, Matérn) with nugget and range bounds.

bullets: Best fit prefers Matérn when available; saves diagnostic comparison of empirical points to fitted curves.
paragraphs: The variogram step grounds later kriging: it shows how height differences grow with distance and whether a single isotropic model is a reasonable first pass.

figures.caption: Empirical semivariogram with fitted models. Informs range shape, nugget, and whether lag settings are sensible.

sections.title: Universal kriging surface
bullets: Universal kriging with linear drift on a regular grid; stratified conditioning oversamples tall buildings when subsampling for runtime.

segments.text: Outputs predicted
and kriging variance per cell: confident cores versus uncertain ribbons at data-sparse edges.
Read the two panels together: high
where data support exists, and variance where the map extrapolates.

figures.caption: Predicted height surface (left) and prediction variance (right). Joint view of signal versus uncertainty.

segments.text: Covariates include zoning joins, optional GTFS and Census features, and planar basis terms on the stacked point and grid set. A HistGradientBoostingRegressor fits the mean trend; residuals feed a new variogram and ordinary kriging of residuals. Grid prediction is

Workplace jobs from LEHD WAC are summed into grid cells; maps use km offsets from the grid corner for readability. Two-year snapshot compares
fields to show where employment mass sits relative to the height story.

Regularized map:

sections.title: Reading the outputs
bullets: Exact CV RMSE, ranks, and top labels are run-specific and depend on your footprint extract and LEHD years.
Compare unregularized and regularized future maps: the latter emphasizes geography away from the strongest cluster-potential seeds (per percentiles and distance decay).
Area labels are nearest to fixed lon/lat anchors, not municipal boundaries; cross-check with map coordinates and trust-distance fields when present.

projects.title: Clean Your Dishes
bullets: Detects and classifies dirty dishes in real-time using YOLO/DINO models through a camera placed in the kitchen.
Uses timestamps to track the sequence of dishes and their states, triggering an alert when a dish has been left dirty for over 30 minutes.

intro: A lightweight CV pipeline watches the sink area, classifies dish state (clean / dirty / ambiguous), and maintains a per-dish timeline from detections.
Alerts fire when dirty dwell time crosses a threshold—tunable for roommates or shared kitchens.
detail.stack: Camera stream → frame sampling → detector (YOLO or DINO-based detector) → tracker or heuristic ID stitching → timer service → notification channel.
detail.methods: Data augmentation for kitchen lighting; confidence thresholds to reduce false alarms; optional temporal smoothing across frames.

visuals.caption: Bounding boxes on sink scene + timer overlay (screenshot).
